title: Spoken Kannada Kids Curriculum Proposal
purpose: A reviewable replacement/overlay curriculum for children learning spoken Kannada from English.
design_notes: Start with high-frequency real-life classroom and home phrases, not adult phrasebook topics.
Use short call-and-response exchanges that a child can act out immediately.
Keep each chapter focused on one communicative goal and a small reusable vocabulary set.
Prefer natural spoken Kannada over literal English translation.
Include Kannada script, learner-friendly romanization, and original English intent for each line.
Dialogues should sound like real kids talking, not like grammar worksheets.
Each conversation still carries concepts_taught so we know what language idea it practices.
Tense practice should appear inside scenes: food, games, car rides, school, parks, and playdates.

strengths: The source has many paired English/Kannada lines and broad everyday topics.
It already contains useful categories such as introductions, home, food, health, cinema, shopping, and travel.
The side-by-side format works well for visual sentence practice.
issues_for_kids: The first chapter jumps into third-person references like he/she/this person/that person before children have enough personal survival phrases.
Many scenarios are adult-heavy: rent, lawyers, office situations, formal visitors, and old-fashioned hotel dialogue.
Some English prompts are unnatural for children today, for example 'How do you do?' or overly formal 'be seated'.
Several OCR romanizations are noisy, so they are not safe as practice targets without correction.
The chapters are long; kids usually need smaller scenes with repetition, choice, and familiar settings.

chapters.title: New friend, ice cream, and names
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Start with the normal first-meeting pattern, but immediately make it fun with ice cream and favorite flavors.

conversations.title: Meeting a new friend at the ice cream shop
conversations.goal: Meet someone new and talk about favorite flavors

lines.speaker: Raju
lines.english: Hi, are you in my class?

lines.speaker: Meena
lines.english: Yes! I sit near the window.

conversations.title: After the ice cream
conversations.goal: Say goodbye naturally after doing something fun

chapters.title: How are you, and what do you want to do?
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Feelings become more engaging when they connect to games, snacks, and play plans.

conversations.title: Choosing a game at recess
conversations.goal: Talk about mood through a real play plan

conversations.title: Taking a break after playing
conversations.goal: Say tired, ask for water, and decide what happens next

chapters.title: Family, car drive, and weekend fun
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Family introductions are more memorable when tied to a weekend car ride or fun outing.

conversations.title: Family photo before a car drive
conversations.goal: Introduce family and talk about a weekend plan

lines.speaker: Kiran
lines.english: Yes, we’re going for a car drive.

conversations.title: Everyone wants to come
conversations.goal: Use he/she/we while planning to go out

lines.speaker: Maya
lines.english: Great, we can all play after lunch.

chapters.title: Classroom games and asking again
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Classroom survival phrases are easier when the scene includes a game and a friendly teacher.

conversations.title: Teacher explains a word game
conversations.goal: Follow classroom directions in a fun game

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.english: Say your name and one food you like.

conversations.title: Say it one more time
conversations.goal: Ask for repetition without feeling embarrassed

chapters.title: My toys, books, and game board
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Object words should start with things kids care about: toys, books, pencils, and games.

conversations.title: Finding the game board
conversations.goal: Use this/that with toys and games

lines.speaker: Child
lines.english: That’s my Snake and Ladder board.

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.english: Do you want to play now?

conversations.title: Finding crayons before drawing
conversations.goal: Ask where things are using here/there/under

lines.speaker: Nila
lines.english: Oh, thanks. Where’s the red crayon?

chapters.title: Pasta, ice cream, and snacks
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Food is one of the fastest ways to get kids speaking because they have strong preferences.

conversations.title: Pasta for dinner
conversations.goal: Ask for food naturally and use polite requests

lines.speaker: Mom
lines.english: A little cheese or a lot?

conversations.title: Ice cream after dinner
conversations.goal: Choose a flavor and say yes/no naturally

lines.speaker: Dad
lines.english: Do you want a big scoop?

lines.speaker: Friend
lines.english: Are you eating pasta again now?

lines.speaker: Friend
lines.english: Will you eat pasta tomorrow too?

lines.speaker: Friend
lines.english: Can I join you next time?

chapters.title: Games we like
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Preference language is more exciting when kids talk about board games, outdoor games, and winning.

conversations.title: Snake and Ladder
conversations.goal: Invite a friend to play and talk during the game

lines.speaker: Arjun
lines.english: Sara, want to play Snake and Ladder?

lines.speaker: Arjun
lines.english: Oh no, I landed on a snake.

lines.speaker: Sara
lines.english: Try again. You can catch up.

conversations.title: What should we play?
conversations.goal: Choose a game with a friend

lines.speaker: Riya
lines.english: What do you want to play?

lines.speaker: Mohan
lines.english: Fine. You hide near the tree.

lines.speaker: Arjun
lines.english: Yes, I played Snake and Ladder.

chapters.title: Where is the candy?
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Location words become fun when kids search for candy, toys, or a ball.

conversations.title: Finding the hidden candy
conversations.goal: Use where/here/there/under/behind in a fun search

lines.speaker: Child
lines.english: Where did you hide the candy?

chapters.title: Can I play?
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Permission phrases are practical when kids ask to play, borrow, go outside, or get a treat.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.english: Teacher, can I go outside for a minute?

lines.speaker: Nina
lines.english: Dev, can I use your red crayon?

chapters.title: Counting candies and birthdays
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Numbers are more memorable with candies, snacks, birthdays, and game scores.

lines.speaker: Mom
lines.english: How many candies do you have?

lines.speaker: Mom
lines.english: Can you give one to your sister?

chapters.title: Morning routine
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Routine language gives repeatable daily practice at home.

conversations.title: Wake up, sleepyhead
conversations.goal: Morning routine in natural home language

chapters.title: Polite words
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Please, sorry, thanks, and excuse me are high-value social phrases.

lines.speaker: Arun
lines.english: Oops, sorry! I bumped into you.

chapters.title: Clothes for a fun day out
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Clothes and colors matter when kids are getting ready for a party or outing.

chapters.title: Body and health
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Keeps the later curriculum useful while making the context child-centered and image-friendly.

chapters.title: Weather
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Keeps the later curriculum useful while making the context child-centered and image-friendly.

chapters.title: Animals
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Keeps the later curriculum useful while making the context child-centered and image-friendly.

chapters.title: At the park: slides, swings, and games
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Outdoor play gives natural reasons to use come, go, run, wait, and again.

conversations.title: Yesterday, now, and tomorrow: park play
conversations.goal: Practice past, present, and future using the same fun topic.

lines.speaker: Child
lines.english: I went to the park yesterday.

lines.speaker: Child
lines.english: I am playing at the park now.

lines.speaker: Child
lines.english: I will go to the park tomorrow.

lines.speaker: Child
lines.english: Yes, we will do it together.

chapters.title: Buying candy at a shop
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Small shopping scenes are fun and teach price, want, one, two, and thank you.

conversations.title: At a shop
conversations.goal: Ask price and buy one item

chapters.title: Directions
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Keeps the later curriculum useful while making the context child-centered and image-friendly.

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.english: It is there, on the left.

chapters.title: Bus ride, car drive, and going places
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Transportation language is more fun when kids choose between bus, car, and a day trip.

conversations.title: Yesterday, now, and tomorrow: car drive
conversations.goal: Practice past, present, and future using the same fun topic.

lines.speaker: Child
lines.english: I went on a car drive yesterday.

lines.speaker: Child
lines.english: I am sitting in the car now.

lines.speaker: Child
lines.english: I will go on a car drive tomorrow.

lines.speaker: Child
lines.english: Yes, we will do it together.

chapters.title: Library
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Keeps the later curriculum useful while making the context child-centered and image-friendly.

lines.speaker: Librarian
lines.english: Sure, take it and read it.

chapters.title: Screen time
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Keeps the later curriculum useful while making the context child-centered and image-friendly.

chapters.title: Homework
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Keeps the later curriculum useful while making the context child-centered and image-friendly.

chapters.title: Helping at home
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Keeps the later curriculum useful while making the context child-centered and image-friendly.

chapters.title: Festival sweets and family fun
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Festival scenes combine relatives, sweets, greetings, and excitement.

chapters.title: Phone call
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Keeps the later curriculum useful while making the context child-centered and image-friendly.

chapters.title: Planning a playdate
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Kids often need language for visiting a friend, time, games, and snacks.

lines.speaker: Friend
lines.english: Will you come to my house?

chapters.title: Story words
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Keeps the later curriculum useful while making the context child-centered and image-friendly.

chapters.title: Review: meeting people
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Keeps the later curriculum useful while making the context child-centered and image-friendly.

lines.speaker: Dev
lines.english: My name is Dev. How are you?

chapters.title: Review: asking for things
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Keeps the later curriculum useful while making the context child-centered and image-friendly.

chapters.title: Big review: playground adventure
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: The final review should feel like a mini story with friends, games, snacks, and movement.

lines.speaker: Maya
lines.english: Hi Ravi, do you want to play?

conversations.title: Big tense review: candy, games, and park
conversations.goal: Practice past, present, and future using the same fun topic.

lines.speaker: Child
lines.english: I am playing a game now.

lines.speaker: Child
lines.english: I will go to the park tomorrow.

lines.speaker: Child
lines.english: Yes, we will do it together.

grammar_spiral.purpose: Repeat a small set of grammar ideas through fun kid conversations instead of teaching grammar as isolated rules.

targets.label: Future / later
examples: We will go to the park.

targets.label: Here / there / this / that
targets.kid_prompt: Point and say where it is.

targets.label: I / you / he / she / we / they
People / places / things
examples: The candy is on the table.

grammar_spiral.spiral_rule: Every 2-3 chapters should include at least one line that contrasts now, before, and later, or here/there, or he/she/they.
